segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a hand placing a dark bottle on a wooden bar, followed by a historical painting depicting people in period clothing drinking from mugs, suggesting a long-standing cultural association with alcohol. The scene transitions to a modern bar setting where a man in a blue shirt gestures toward a small glass of beer, with text overlay asking, 'Is this amount of alcohol dangerous?' The narrative then shifts to a split-screen interview between Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses in a plaid shirt, and Joss, a woman with dark hair in a tie-dye top, both speaking into microphones. The video cuts to a World Health Organization news release stating 'No level of alcohol consumption is safe for our health,' followed by a New York Times headline about alcohol labels warning of cancer risk. The scene changes to a TV in a living room showing a news anchor from 'Today Show Australia' discussing alcohol, then to a vintage TV in a rustic room displaying Canadian alcohol guidelines from 2023, with graphics showing '10 standard drinks per week' and gender-specific limits. The video includes street interviews with people reacting to alcohol news, and ends with a news anchor from '9 News Australia' discussing alcohol in front of a backdrop of bottles and drinks, while another anchor from 'FOX News' reports on red wine being equivalent to an hour of gym time. The setting shifts between modern studios, homes, and historical imagery, with lighting varying from warm bar tones to bright newsroom lights, emphasizing the global and ongoing debate about alcohol's health impacts.

segments.caption: The video opens with a split-screen interview featuring Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses in a blue plaid shirt, speaking into a silver microphone in a room with shelves holding a green rotary phone, keyboard, and globe, and Joss, a woman with dark hair and bangs in a blue and white tie-dye top, speaking into a black microphone in a room with a plant and orange couch. The scene transitions to a man with curly hair and glasses, wearing a dark blue button-up shirt, sitting at a wooden table in a dimly lit bar, holding a newspaper and gesturing as he speaks. The camera then focuses on a close-up of the newspaper, revealing the title 'Alcohol and Mortality' and a study from the 'Kaiser Permanente Experience.' This is followed by a 3D animated visualization of data, showing rows of bottle caps arranged by daily alcohol consumption categories (0, ≤2, 3-5, 6+ drinks/day), with corresponding mortality rates represented by colored spheres (gray for deceased, yellow for alive). The animation highlights that the group consuming 3-5 drinks per day has the highest survival rate, while the 6+ group shows the highest mortality. The average age of 44.7 is displayed for all groups, emphasizing the study's controlled variables. The segment concludes with a return to the man in the bar, who continues to speak while holding the newspaper, reinforcing the theme of healthy moderation in alcohol consumption.

segments.caption: The video begins with a 3D animated visualization titled 'number of drinks/day,' displaying four groups of bottle caps arranged on colored platforms (black, light blue, purple, red) corresponding to consumption levels: 0, ≤2, 3-5, and 6+ drinks per day. As the animation progresses, skulls appear beneath the 3-5 and 6+ groups, with mortality percentages (9.3% and 12.7%) displayed. A hand then appears, picking up a bottle cap, symbolizing individual consumption. The scene transitions to text defining 'binge drinking' (4+ for women, 5+ for men) and 'heavy drinking' (8+ for women, 15+ for men). The mortality rates are updated to 8.8%, 6.3%, 9.3%, and 12.7% for the respective groups. Next, bar graphs appear for 'accidents,' 'liver disease,' and 'cancer,' showing the highest deaths in the 6+ group, with annotations emphasizing 'highest for heavy drinkers' and 'lowest for moderate drinkers.' A new bar graph for 'cardiac events' shows the highest deaths in the 0 group, labeled 'highest for non-drinkers.' This is followed by a scatter plot of mortality rate vs. drinks/day, highlighting a U-shaped curve with the lowest mortality at ≤2 drinks/day. The scene then cuts to a split-screen interview with Adam and Joss, both speaking into microphones in their respective rooms. Adam is in a room with shelves containing a green rotary phone, keyboard, and globe, while Joss is in a room with a plant and orange couch. The video ends with a close-up of a scientific paper titled 'Gaziano et al. Alcohol and Mortality,' showing a U-shaped curve for 'Total Mortality' and 'CVD Mortality' against 'Current Alcohol Consumption (Drinks/Week),' with a note about the study's methodology and hazard ratios.

active_entities.3D Data Visualization: An animated graphic displaying bottle caps arranged by daily alcohol consumption categories (0, ≤2, 3-5, 6+ drinks/day), with colored spheres (gray for deceased, yellow for alive) indicating mortality rates, and text showing average age of 44.7 for all groups.

segments.caption: The video begins with a close-up of a scientific paper titled 'Di Castelnuovo et al. Wine and Beer Intake and Vascular Risk,' displaying a graph of relative risk versus wine intake, suggesting a J-shaped curve. This transitions to a stylized animated J-curve graph on a blue background, labeled 'the J-curve,' showing relative risk of death decreasing with low alcohol intake before rising again. The scene shifts to a split-screen interview with Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses in a blue plaid shirt, speaking into a silver microphone in a room with shelves containing a green rotary phone and keyboard, and Joss, a woman with dark hair and bangs in a blue and white tie-dye top, speaking into a black microphone in a room with a plant and orange couch. The video then shows a graph of 'US wine consumption' in gallons per person from 1980 to 2020, with a '60 Minutes' logo appearing over the dip in the 1990s. This is followed by a vintage TV screen displaying the text '... alcohol reduces the risk of heart disease ...' over grainy footage. The next segment features Tanya Chikritzhs, a woman with short dark hair and glasses, wearing a red cardigan over a white collared shirt, speaking from a room with a blue abstract painting behind her. She appears in a split-screen with Adam, who is now wearing a maroon shirt. The video then shows a close-up of a scientific paper titled 'Error in Prospective Studies' by Tanya Chikritzhs, Kaye Fillmore, and William Kerr. The final frames include a painting of a man in historical attire holding a wine glass, with the text '#1 - issues with the J-curve' and a number '2' overlay, followed by Tanya Chikritzhs speaking again, gesturing with her hand.

segments.caption: The video begins with a close-up of a scientific paper discussing alcohol consumption, highlighting a question about drinking habits and the number of drinks per day. The scene transitions to Tanya Chikritzhs, who is speaking from a room with a blue abstract painting behind her, wearing a red cardigan over a white collared shirt. She gestures while explaining the data. The video then cuts to a split-screen showing Tanya Chikritzhs on the right and Adam on the left, who is now wearing a maroon shirt and speaking into a silver microphone in a room with shelves containing a green rotary phone, keyboard, and globe. The scene shifts to a dark, painted image of a skull smoking a cigarette, symbolizing the dangers of substance use. Returning to the split-screen, Tanya continues her explanation, emphasizing points with hand gestures. The video then displays a 3D data visualization with bottle caps arranged by daily alcohol consumption categories (0, ≤2, 3-5, 6+ drinks/day), with green caps indicating 'former drinkers' and 'sick abstainers' within the '0' category. A graph appears, showing mortality rates increasing with higher alcohol consumption, with a notable spike at 0 drinks/day due to sick abstainers. The segment concludes with a historical painting of a man holding a wine glass, overlaid with text reading '2 issues with the J-curve #2 - rich drinkers,' suggesting a discussion on socioeconomic factors in alcohol research.

segments.caption: The video opens with a split-screen interview featuring Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses wearing a plaid shirt, speaking into a silver microphone in a room with shelves displaying a green rotary phone, keyboard, and globe, and Joss, a woman with dark hair and bangs in a blue and white tie-dye top, speaking into a black microphone in a room with a plant and orange couch. The scene transitions to a close-up of a smartphone on a wooden table, showing a man in a gray t-shirt and cap delivering a message with subtitles: 'if I die anytime soon... I want you to call it Hank’s Razor... for anything that can be explained by socioeconomic status in a society... that’s probably that rather than the thing that you’re measuring... and I’m not gonna die anytime soon.' The video then returns to the split-screen with Adam and Joss, both smiling and engaged. Adam then appears alone, holding a printed document, as a scientific paper titled 'Lifetime income patterns and alcohol consumption' is displayed on screen, highlighting the finding: 'Lower income was associated with higher odds of abstinence and of heavy drinking, relative to light/moderate drinking.' This is followed by a 3D data visualization showing bottle caps arranged by daily alcohol consumption (0, ≤2, 3-5, 6+ drinks/day), with dollar signs appearing on some caps to indicate socioeconomic status. The segment concludes with a historical painting of a man in 17th-century attire holding a wine glass, overlaid with the text '#1 - issues with the J-curve' and a number '2', suggesting a thematic connection to alcohol consumption patterns and societal factors.

segments.caption: The video begins with a richly detailed historical painting of a lavish banquet, featuring a lobster, fruits, and meats, setting a tone of indulgence. This transitions to a close-up of a scientific paper discussing alcohol consumption and mortality, highlighting text about educational attainment and health lifestyles. A simple scatter plot then appears, showing mortality rates against daily drink consumption, with a yellow arrow pointing to the lowest rate at ≤2 drinks/day. The scene shifts to the Bar Speaker, a man with curly hair and glasses in a dark blue shirt, sitting at a wooden table in a dimly lit bar, speaking directly to the camera. He gestures as he explains, and a small glass of amber liquid appears on the table. A J-curve graph is overlaid, illustrating the relative risk of death versus drinks per day. The video then cuts to a sponsorship screen for 'GROUND News,' followed by a smartphone interface displaying various news articles. The screen zooms into a detailed bias and factuality analysis tool, showing media sources categorized by political lean and factual accuracy. The scene transitions to a split-screen video call: on the left, a woman with dark hair and earphones speaks into a black microphone in a room with a plant and orange couch; on the right, the Bar Speaker, now in a plaid shirt, speaks into a silver microphone in a room with shelves containing a green rotary phone, keyboard, and globe. The video ends with a website interface for 'GROUND News' showing 'Blindspot' sections for Left and Right, highlighting news stories with minimal coverage from one political side, accompanied by bar graphs indicating media bias distribution.

segments.caption: The video begins with a series of news headlines displayed on a dark interface, each accompanied by a bias analysis bar graph showing percentages for Left, Center, and Right sources. The first headline reads 'Senior DOJ officials leaked investigation details before election: Report,' with 89% of sources categorized as Right. The next headline, 'World’s 500 Richest Surpassed $10 Trillion in Wealth: Bloomberg,' shows 75% of sources as Left. The scene then transitions to a split-screen video call featuring a woman with dark hair and bangs wearing earphones and a black shirt, and a man with curly hair and glasses wearing a patterned shirt, speaking into a silver microphone in a room with a yellow cabinet and books. The man gestures while speaking, and the woman listens attentively. The video then cuts to a black screen with a QR code and the URL 'ground.news/howtown,' followed by a promotional offer for '40% OFF Vantage Plan.' The scene shifts to a dimly lit bar where the same man, now in a dark blue button-up shirt, sits at a wooden table with a small glass of beer, gesturing as he speaks. A close-up of a scientific paper titled 'Association Between Daily Alcohol Intake and Risk of All-Cause Mortality' is shown, followed by an animated J-curve graph illustrating the risk of death versus number of drinks per day, with the curve dipping below 1.0 risk at low consumption levels before rising sharply. The man in the bar continues to explain the graph, using hand gestures to emphasize his points, while the graph remains overlaid on the screen.

segments.caption: The video begins with Tanya Chikritzhs, a woman with short dark hair and glasses, wearing a red cardigan over a white collared shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a room with a blue abstract painting behind her. She gestures with her hands as she discusses a scientific paper, which is then shown in close-up: a study titled 'Alcohol use and burden for 195 countries and territories, 1990–2016: a systematic analysis for the Global Burden of Disease Study 2016.' The video transitions to a world map illustrating current drinker prevalence by region, followed by a series of animated graphs on a blue background. These graphs depict the risk of death from ischaemic heart disease, cirrhosis, and all attributable health outcomes in relation to the number of drinks per day, with a J-shaped curve for heart disease and a steadily increasing risk for other outcomes. The scene shifts to a man with curly hair and glasses, the Bar Speaker, sitting at a wooden table in a dimly lit bar with a small glass of beer, speaking to the camera. The video then cuts to a split-screen interview with Adam, wearing a plaid shirt and speaking into a silver microphone, and Joss, wearing a blue and white tie-dye top and speaking into a black microphone, both in their respective home settings. The segment concludes with an animated visualization of a randomized controlled trial, showing bottle caps being assigned to 'drink' or 'don’t drink' groups by a researcher’s hand, symbolizing the experimental design.

segments.caption: The video begins with an animated visualization of a randomized controlled trial, where a hand labeled 'researcher' assigns bottle caps to two groups: 'drink' (on a red card) and 'don’t drink' (on a black card), illustrating the design of a clinical study. The scene transitions to a split-screen video call featuring Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses in a plaid shirt speaking into a silver microphone, and Joss, a woman with dark hair and bangs in a blue and white tie-dye top, speaking into a black microphone. They appear to be discussing the trial, with Adam gesturing as he speaks. The animation returns, showing the 'researcher' hand pointing to a red card labeled '5 drinks/day' and then to a single bottle cap with the number '60' appearing beside it, symbolizing a daily consumption metric. The video then cuts to a close-up of a scientific paper titled 'The Moderate Alcohol and Cardiovascular Health Trial (MACH15): Design and methods for a randomized trial of moderate alcohol consumption and cardiometabolic risk,' with text highlighting 'one alcoholic drink daily over an average of six years.' The scene alternates back to Adam and Joss, who continue their discussion, with Joss listening intently and occasionally adjusting her earphones. The overall tone is informative, blending animated data visualization with a live conversation between two experts.

segments.caption: The video opens with a split-screen interview featuring Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses in a plaid shirt, speaking into a silver microphone in a room with shelves of eclectic items, and Joss, a woman with dark hair and bangs in a blue and white tie-dye top, speaking into a black microphone in a room with a plant and orange couch. The scene transitions to a close-up of a New York Times article titled 'Federal Agency Courted Alcohol Industry to Fund Study on Benefits of Moderate Drinking,' highlighting text about Anheuser Busch InBev, Heineken, and other alcohol companies funding a $100 million NIH study through donations to a private foundation. The article also mentions that scientists gave talks suggesting the study would endorse moderate drinking as healthy. The video then cuts back to Adam and Joss, followed by a scene of the Bar Speaker, a man with curly hair and glasses in a dark blue shirt, sitting at a wooden table in a dimly lit bar with a small glass of beer, gesturing as he speaks. This is followed by an animated graphic showing two hands labeled 'genetics researchers' and 'researchers' with a thumbs-down gesture. The video then shows a historical painting of a group of people, overlaid with the number '4' and the text 'Mendelian randomization,' followed by a portrait of Gregor Mendel. An animated sequence of purple and white flowers appears, symbolizing genetic variation, before returning to the Bar Speaker in the bar setting, continuing his explanation.

segments.caption: The video begins with Kiran Biddinger from Princeton University speaking directly to the camera in a well-lit room with a bookshelf behind him, featuring neatly arranged books and framed photos. The scene transitions to a split-screen interview with Adam and Joss, both speaking into microphones in their respective home environments—Adam in a room with eclectic shelves including a green rotary phone and keyboard, and Joss in a bright space with a plant and orange couch. The video then cuts to a YouTube interface showing a Vox video titled 'Asian flush, explained,' featuring a diagram of alcohol metabolism with labels for ADH and ALDH2 enzymes, and a visual of three people drinking. The scene returns to Adam and Joss, who continue their discussion, with Joss gesturing as she speaks. The video then displays a world map with color gradients indicating the prevalence of a genetic trait, with darker blue regions (more common) concentrated in East Asia. This is followed by an animated graphic of bottle caps, some turning red and purple to represent 'drink less' and 'drink more' categories. The final scene shows the Bar Speaker in a dimly lit bar, gesturing while speaking at a wooden table with a small glass of beer, surrounded by ambient bar lighting and decor.

segments.caption: The video begins with the Bar Speaker, a man with curly hair and glasses in a dark blue button-up shirt, sitting at a wooden table in a dimly lit bar, gesturing as he speaks. The scene transitions to a split-screen showing Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses in a plaid shirt, speaking into a silver microphone in a room with eclectic shelves, and Kiran Biddinger, a man with dark hair in a dark t-shirt, speaking in front of a bookshelf. The video then cuts to an animated 3D Data Visualization of bottle caps being sorted into 'drink more' and 'drink less' categories by a green hand labeled 'genetics.' This is followed by a close-up of a Scientific Paper Visualization titled 'Association of Habitual Alcohol Intake With Risk of Cardiovascular Disease' from JAMA Network Open, with text highlighting 'n=371 463' and 'genetic ancestry.' Kiran Biddinger reappears, explaining the data, followed by a graph showing 'worse' and 'better' outcomes for hypertension, coronary artery disease, heart attack, and stroke, with red dots for 'drink more' and black dots for 'drink less.' The graph transitions to a logarithmic scale showing odds ratio increasing with drinks per day for coronary artery disease. The final scene shows a split-screen with Adam on the left and Joss, a woman with dark hair and bangs in a blue and white tie-dye top, speaking into a black microphone in a bright room with a plant and orange couch in the background.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a glass of beer, showcasing golden liquid with rising bubbles and condensation, setting a thematic tone around alcohol. The scene transitions to a split-screen video call featuring Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses in a plaid shirt, speaking into a silver microphone in a room with eclectic shelves including a green rotary phone and keyboard, and Kiran Biddinger, a man with dark hair in a black t-shirt, speaking in front of a bookshelf with framed photos and books. They engage in a lively conversation, with Adam gesturing and Kiran smiling and nodding. The video then cuts to a full-screen view of Kiran, who continues speaking with expressive hand movements. The scene shifts back to the split-screen, now including Joss, a woman with dark hair and bangs in a blue and white tie-dye top, speaking into a black microphone in a room with a plant and orange couch. The video then cuts to a dimly lit bar where the Bar Speaker, a man with curly hair and glasses in a dark blue shirt, sits at a wooden table with a small glass of alcohol and a document, gesturing as he speaks. The camera zooms in on the document, revealing a scientific paper titled 'Alcohol use and burden for 195 countries and territories, 1990–2016: a systematic analysis for the Global Burden of Disease Study 2016,' with close-ups of text listing health risks like 'Esophageal cancer,' 'Ischaemic stroke,' and 'Tuberculosis.' The segment ends with a black screen featuring a single bottle cap, followed by a grid of white dots forming a pattern, suggesting a data visualization or transition.

segments.caption: The video begins with a data visualization showing 100,000 non-drinkers, with 914 (0.91%) highlighted in green as developing alcohol-related diseases. The visualization transitions to 100,000 light drinkers, with 919 developing such diseases, then to 100,000 moderate drinkers, with 979 affected, and finally to 100,000 heavy drinkers, with 1,251 affected. The numbers increase progressively, emphasizing rising risk with consumption. The scene then cuts to Joss, a woman with dark hair and bangs, wearing a blue and white tie-dye top, speaking into a black microphone in a room with a plant and orange couch. Next, Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses in a plaid shirt, appears speaking into a silver microphone in a room with eclectic shelves, including a green rotary phone and keyboard. The video returns to the data visualization, now showing a graph titled 'all attributable health outcomes' with a red curve rising from 0 to 14 drinks per day, indicating increasing risk. The scene shifts to the Bar Speaker, a man with curly hair and glasses in a dark blue button-up shirt, sitting at a wooden table in a dimly lit bar with a glass of amber liquid, gesturing as he speaks. The lighting is warm and ambient, with wooden decor and a bar counter in the background.

segments.caption: The video opens with the Bar Speaker, a man with curly hair and glasses in a dark blue button-up shirt, seated at a wooden table in a dimly lit bar, gesturing as he speaks. The scene transitions to a close-up of a frothy beer mug on a wooden surface, followed by a series of newspaper clippings with headlines promoting the benefits of moderate drinking, including 'Alcohol Labels Should Warn of Cancer Risk, Surgeon General Says' and 'Moderate drinking shows benefits.' The sequence continues with a black-and-white newspaper headline 'BOOZE BENEFITS' from June 12, 2000, and then shifts to a split-screen interview featuring Adam, who is speaking into a silver microphone in a room with eclectic shelves, and Joss, who listens attentively into a black microphone in a room with a plant and orange couch. The video then cuts to Tanya Chikritzhs, a woman with short dark hair and glasses in a red cardigan, speaking animatedly in a room with a blue abstract painting behind her. The scene returns to Adam and Joss in the split-screen format, then back to the Bar Speaker in the bar. A graph titled 'all attributable health outcomes' appears, showing a rising red line indicating increased risk with more drinks per day. The video concludes with a painting of two men at a table, one slumped over, and then a vibrant painting of a group toasting with champagne in a sunlit outdoor setting.

segments.caption: The video begins with a split-screen interview featuring Adam, a man with curly hair and glasses wearing a plaid shirt, speaking into a silver microphone in a room filled with eclectic items like a green rotary phone and a keyboard, and Joss, a woman with dark hair and bangs in a blue and white tie-dye top, speaking into a black microphone in a bright room with a plant and orange couch. Joss gestures expressively while speaking, and Adam smiles and nods in response. The scene transitions to a full-screen view of Joss, who continues to gesture and adjust her earphones. The video then cuts to Adam in a dimly lit bar, now wearing a dark blue button-up shirt, seated at a wooden table, sipping from a small glass and gesturing as he speaks. On-screen text appears, crediting Adam Cole and Joss Fong as writers and producers, followed by a 'SUBSCRIBE' graphic with a bird logo and a Patreon link. The screen then displays the 'Science paper Book Club' title over a green background, followed by the Alfred P. Sloan Foundation logo. The final frames show a webpage for the 'Public Understanding of Science & Technology' program, highlighting sections like Books, Film, New Media, Radio, Special Initiatives, and Television, with images of books, people, and events.
